title: New Campagnolo Record gets a 13-speed upgrade with trickle-down from Super Record 13
content:
Cyclist
New Campagnolo Record gets a 13-speed upgrade with trickle-down from Super Record 13

Campagnolo has trickled down its tech from Super Record 13 to a lower priced Record 13 groupset, lowering the flagship 2×13-speed groupset price from €4,300 (approx £3,720) for Super Record to €2,699 (approx £2,340) for Record. The simplified Record power meter is also less expensive at €600 (£520) versus €1,100 (£950) for Super Record. 

A full range of road, all-road and gravel 1x and 2x Record groupset configurations is available, mirroring Super Record. 

According to Campagnolo, ‘Record 13 and Super Record 13 share the same platform, the same architecture, the same origin. They are cut from the same stone. Record 13 is a decision, not a compromise, but a deliberate expression of engineering intelligence. Designed for cycling, however you ride.’

Campagnolo Record 13 and Super Record 13 share the same design features, many of their subcomponents and their electronics, allowing you to mix Record and Super Record components. Campagnolo says that the shifting speed of Record is also identical to Super Record 13.

As with previous generations of Record, the materials used are less pricey than Super Record, which Campagnolo says results in a system weight that’s between 208g and 342g greater.

Configurations, weights and prices

While Campagnolo introduced Super Record in two tranches, starting with the 2x option then 1x and gravel variants (plus the Ultra upgrade kit in between), Record has been announced in all five configurations, although two options will become available in July rather than immediately:

  • Record 2×13 Road – 2,783g claimed weight, €2,699
  • Record 1×13 Road – 2,656g claimed weight, €2,335
  • Record 2×13 All Road – 2,806g claimed weight, €2,765

All the above can be used with two new Record cassettes with 10-33t and 11-36t ranges, as well as the existing Super Record and Ultra cassettes.

  • Record X 1×13 Road – 2,820g claimed weight, €2,129
  • Record X 1×13 Gravel – 2,777g claimed weight, €2,129

These two groupsets are compatible with 9-42t and 10-48t cassettes, although only the 10-48t configuration is available at launch.

All except the 2×13 and 1×13 road configurations include Campagnolo’s Nano Clutch, also seen on equivalent Super Record 13 groupsets and designed to assist chain retention.

The 2×13 Road, 1×13 Road X and 1×13 Gravel X groupsets are available immediately, the 1×13 Road and 2×13 All Road groupsets will be sold from July.

Multiple crankset options

There are the same seven 2x chainring pairs as for Super Record 13 – in fact the two groupsets use identical chainrings: 45/29t, 48/32t, 50/34t, 52/36t, 53/39t, 54/39t and 55/39t.

In addition, there are eight 1x aero chainring sizes: 38t, 40t, 42t, 44t, 46t, 48t, 50t and 52t.

The crank arms of Record are still carbon, but rather than being hollow, they incorporate a low density technopolymer. Campagnolo doesn’t offer quite the range of crank arm lengths of SRAM and Shimano, with 165mm, 170mm and 172.5mm crank lengths available. 

SRAM Force and Shimano Ultegra are both sold in additional 160mm and 175mm lengths. 

As with previous generations of Record, there’s a stainless steel crank spindle in place of Super Record 13’s titanium, although Campagnolo says that it has been able to take advantage of steel’s higher strength to reduce the spindle thickness and hence limit the additional weight. 

There are 1x and 2x power meter options, which Campagnolo says use simplified electronics, while still offering +/-2% power accuracy, versus +/-1% for the Super Record HPPM power meter. But the Record power meter adds €600 to the groupset price, compared to the €1,100 additional price for the Super Record power meter.

The Record rear derailleur body is made of carbon fibre-reinforced polyamide, while there’s an internal steel cage to ensure robustness. There are stainless steel bearings to the 14-tooth jockey wheels, which have narrow/wide teeth, which Campagnolo says ensures durability and quiet running. The Record X configurations include 16-tooth lower and 12-tooth upper jockey wheels in a long cage design.

Thumb shifting remains

Also trickled down from Super Record are the lever ergonomics, with thumb shifters and a third, configurable Smart Button on the hoods. The brakes’ hydraulic system is the same as Super Record, but the hoods have a new gloss/matt grip pattern.

The brake callipers too are the same as Super Record, just with a new finish. They come fitted with organic pads, but Campagnolo sells sintered pads too, which it claims will offer 10,000km of pad life on dry roads but can also be used for gravel and wet riding conditions. 

Another component that’s identical to Super Record is the derailleur batteries, which Campagnolo claims are good for 750km of riding. They can be charged on-bike or off and have a USB-C cable connector for wide compatibility. 

Finally, Record and Super Record use the same My Campy app for configuration, firmware updates and diagnostics.

As with Super Record 13, Campagnolo continues to produce all the Record 13 groupset components in-house in Italy, which it says ensures quality control over the whole production process.  

The post New Campagnolo Record gets a 13-speed upgrade with trickle-down from Super Record 13 appeared first on Cyclist.
author: Pnorman
